facts_disclosed: Goes to St. Cecilia's every Sunday
Plans to work until 63 mandatory retirement

turns.content: question for you. Marie's birthday is in March and she mentioned wanting to try one of those cooking class things. couples cooking. is that actually fun or is it going to be two hours of me screwing up risotto while some chef yells at me.

Ha — it's usually more fun than you'd expect. Most couples cooking classes are pretty laid back. Nobody's yelling. You'll chop stuff, follow instructions, drink some wine, and eat what you made at the end. It's more of a date night than a real cooking lesson.

What kind of food does Marie like? Italian, French, Asian? And are you looking for something in Philly?
Italian probably. she loves Italian. yeah Philly or close to it. I don't want to be driving an hour for a cooking class.

Good call. There are a few solid options in Philly for Italian couples cooking:

- **Cook** on Headhouse Square — they do hands-on classes, Italian ones come up regularly. Nice space, BYOB.
- **Sur La Table** in King of Prussia — a little bit of a drive but they run a lot of Italian-themed classes. Pasta making, date night Italian, that kind of thing.
- **Cozymeal** — it's a website that connects you with local chefs who host small classes in their own kitchens or studios. You can search specifically for Italian in Philly. More intimate, usually 6-10 people.

For a birthday gift, Cook is probably the best bet. Book a Saturday class, take her to dinner after, done. Clean plan.

Does she know you're looking into this, or is it a surprise?
she brought it up so she knows I'm thinking about it. it's not going to be a surprise. Marie and I don't really do surprises. 32 years you kind of know what's coming.

That's fair — at 32 years the surprises become less about the what and more about actually following through on it. Book it and she'll be happy.

I'd suggest looking at Cook first since it's right in the city. Check their March calendar — Saturday classes fill up, so if you see one you want, grab it now. Does Marie have a specific Saturday preference, or are your weekends pretty open?

Sundays won't work. we go to St. Cecilia's for Mass every week. been our parish forever. Marie would kill me if I skipped it. but Saturdays are good. I'm usually off weekends unless I pick up overtime.

Got it — Saturdays are the window. Most of these classes run in the afternoon or evening, usually 2-4 hours. A Saturday evening class would work well — you cook, you eat what you made, and you're home at a reasonable hour.

Here's what I'd do:
1. Go to the Cook website and look at their March Saturday evening classes
2. Look for anything Italian — fresh pasta, classic Italian date night, that kind of thing
3. Book it for two
4. Tell Marie it's handled

If their March calendar isn't up yet, check back in a few weeks. They usually post a month or two out.
I'll check. speaking of schedules. had my annual review with the deputy chief last week. went fine. the usual. they asked me what my plans are.
same thing I tell them every year. mandatory is 63 so I've got five left. I'm riding it out to the end. no reason to leave early. the job is who I am.

That's a clear answer. Five more years puts you at — what, 63? That's a hell of a career when you get there. Not a lot of guys make it to mandatory.

Did they push back at all or is the review just a formality at this point?
formality. they know where I stand. I'm not one of these guys counting days on a calendar. I still want to be here. the day I don't want to be here I'll know. but that's not today.

That's the right way to look at it. Some guys check out years before they leave. Sounds like you're still in it. Your crew's better for that.

So the plan is: book the cooking class, keep showing up to work, Mass on Sundays. Sounds like you've got a good routine going.
that about covers it. not complicated. I like it that way.
Nothing wrong with simple. Let me know if you need help finding that cooking class — or anything else. Enjoy the weekend.
